video_title: Should You Invest In SpaceX IPO, Elon Musk, Bitcoin or AI?
gist: Jordi Visser frames SpaceX's IPO as two businesses in one: a high-revenue 'put' (Starlink + the fourth-largest hyperscaler via Colossus) plus a massive call option on cheap orbital data centers, while arguing the real AI bottleneck is physical commodities and energy, not models. He sees an AI midcycle slowdown, commoditizing models, Anthropic beating OpenAI, and positions in silver, copper and energy as the under-owned trades.

themes.theme: SpaceX as a dual put-plus-call-option AI/space play

insights.claim: SpaceX should be valued as two companies: a near-term revenue 'put' from Starlink plus the Colossus data-center business (now the fourth-largest hyperscaler), and a long-dated call option on space stations and rapid-reuse rockets.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Investors weighing the SpaceX IPO should separate the cash-flowing base business from the speculative orbital optionality rather than judging it on one multiple.
insights.quote: you're getting a put in it, which is the revenue that's coming in right now. It's still valued extremely rich, but then you're getting this massive call option on these other parts.

insights.claim: SpaceX is now the fourth-largest hyperscaler alongside Amazon, Microsoft and Google, having signed Anthropic and Google as Colossus customers within roughly 30-45 days of having no deals.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Signals SpaceX has near-instant enterprise demand for compute, a revenue stream the market may not yet be pricing into the IPO.

insights.claim: Musk has reportedly secured 20% of all Vera Rubin chip allocations from Nvidia, because Jensen Huang favors the operator already proven to build data centers fastest in a supply-constrained environment.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Privileged chip access is a structural advantage that could let SpaceX/xAI scale compute while rivals are bottlenecked; watch for confirmation in supply commentary.
insights.quote: he has secured 20% of all of the Vera Rubin that'll be going out

insights.claim: An orbital data center could cost ~$5B versus ~$25B for the non-chip portion of a 1-gigawatt terrestrial data center (which runs ~$60B total, ~$35B of it chips), implying dramatically higher compute margins for space-based compute.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If the cost gap holds, orbital compute could be the cheapest marginal capacity, explaining why Google may have invested to lock in rights early.
insights.quote: a data center right now for 1 gawatt is like 60 billion ... about 35 billion is the chips. So you're left with 25 billion. and he's saying he can do the um space station for 5 billion.

insights.claim: Building data centers on land may now be more complicated than building them in space, because orbit removes the cooling, land-acquisition, power and government-regulation bottlenecks that constrain terrestrial buildout.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Inverts the consensus skepticism on orbital data centers; the constraint to watch is terrestrial permitting and power, not the novelty of space.
insights.quote: Building data centers on land might be at this point more complicated than doing them in space.

insights.claim: SpaceX's monopoly on rocket launches creates a durable hardware moat that AI software businesses cannot replicate, since competitors like Google lack the rockets and satellites to compete.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The launch monopoly is the part of the SpaceX thesis least vulnerable to commoditization, unlike the AI layer.
insights.quote: it provides this huge moat that only really exists in the hardware world going forward. Like the AI world, it's going to be very hard to build these modes

insights.claim: SpaceX being worth its current IPO valuation is a low-probability, distribution-of-outcomes bet, but a strong Elon fan base provides a structural bid that limits downside even if milestones slip.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames SpaceX as an asymmetric, low-probability-of-full-payoff bet with a fan-driven price floor rather than a value buy.

insights.claim: If SpaceX reaches thousands of rocket launches a year by end of 2028 (at least three a day, up and back), its satellite dominance becomes near-impossible to challenge and the valuation re-rates higher.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reuse cadence and launch frequency are the key operational KPIs to track for a SpaceX re-rating thesis.

themes.theme: Physical commodities and inputs as the true AI bottleneck

insights.claim: Silver is an under-owned AI/energy bottleneck because both batteries and orbital data-center buildouts need far more of it; Visser is building positions and thinks it could go up 10x.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete contrarian commodity trade tied to the AI buildout that the narrative-chasing crowd is ignoring because it isn't at 52-week highs.
insights.quote: I'll take silver and building a position here in silver and in copper. ... I think it'll go 10 times where it is today.

insights.claim: The copper required over the next 10-15 years equals all the copper mined in the prior 10,000 years, and innovation cannot accelerate getting copper out of the ground.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames copper as a hard supply constraint on the entire AI/electrification buildout, supporting a structurally higher price floor.
insights.quote: the amount of copper we need for the next 10 years is equivalent ... to all the copper mined over the last 10,000 years.

insights.claim: Indium phosphide is a critical mineral required to build all data centers, and China controls essentially all of it and has begun restricting it like it did with rare earths.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Chinese control of indium phosphide is a geopolitical chokepoint on data-center construction; watch Coherent, Lumentum and Marvell supply exposure.
insights.quote: if you go look up indium ... you'll realize it is the critical mineral in all data centers. You can't build data centers without it. And they control it.

insights.claim: China will keep the Strait of Hormuz open because it depends on imported oil from Venezuela, Iran and Russia, tying energy geopolitics to the US-China minerals standoff.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Suggests oil supply disruption fears via Hormuz are overstated, while mineral hoarding remains the real US-China friction point.

insights.claim: Memory (Micron) is a structural AI winner because the shift to an agentic, humanoid-driven world requires off-the-charts memory; Micron rose roughly 10x from early-2024 lows yet is still cheap because analysts underforecast exponential demand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Visser's playbook: when consensus forecasts miss the exponential, a stock can 10x and still look cheap; memory is his template for that.
insights.quote: How is something go up a,000% from where it was during the first quarter of last year and it's still cheap today? Well, for that to happen, everyone's forecast on the exponential needs were wrong.

insights.claim: America's dependence on foreign-controlled chemistry and minerals is far deeper than realized, creating an industrial-sovereignty investing theme exemplified by startups like Athanor targeting critical nitrogen chemicals.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Domestic critical-chemical and reshoring plays are an emerging niche as the list of foreign-controlled inputs keeps growing.

themes.theme: AI model economics, commoditization and the midcycle slowdown

insights.claim: AI labs are selling tokens below their cost of production, subsidizing usage the way VCs once subsidized ride-sharing, so they can show huge revenue while losing money short term.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Unit economics are negative today; the question for pending AI IPOs is whether they can raise prices or cut model costs before investors demand profitability.

insights.claim: Roughly 80-85% of AI queries could be moved off the latest frontier models onto cheap or free Chinese open-source models, squeezing frontier-lab pricing power.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Enterprise routing of routine queries to cheap models caps revenue per token and pressures frontier-model gross margins.

insights.claim: AI models will be commoditized and the cost of producing tokens is rising about 4x year over year, so the binding constraint is compute capacity to sustain ARR, not demand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Capacity-constrained labs will use surge pricing, tiering and ads to ration compute; the bottleneck and pricing dynamics, not model quality, drive near-term economics.
insights.quote: it's like, hey, I we have a thousand people that want to come in the restaurant tonight, but we only have 20 seats. Well, you're not making new seats. So you're in trouble.

insights.claim: DeepSeek's latest model is nearly comparable to frontier models while dramatically cutting price, putting Chinese model stocks into a deflationary sell-off.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Confirms the commoditization/deflation thesis for models; a major story Visser says got under-covered in the prior month.
insights.quote: Deepseek is a major story that did not get any press in the last month.

insights.claim: AI faces stacking political and social headwinds (Bernie Sanders and Trump wanting government ownership stakes, the Pope speaking out) that add 'slow this down' pressure on the sector.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Regulatory and political risk is mounting alongside the economic pressure on model companies, a multi-front headwind for AI equities.
insights.quote: When you put all that together and then you add in Bernie Sanders and Donald Trump want to own part of the companies

insights.claim: AI companies are all rushing to IPO simultaneously (Google raising, Meta planning tens of billions, OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX) because the bubble window may close in 12-18 months.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A wave of simultaneous AI fundraising signals insiders are monetizing while sentiment is hot; treat the IPO rush as a market-timing tell.
insights.quote: Why are they all coming to the market at the same time? ... you go public now or you may not get a chance in 12 months, 18 months

insights.claim: We are in an AI midcycle slowdown for the next 3-6 months where infrastructure, semiconductor and cooling names will see roughly equal disappointment and surprise, because prior growth was inflated by hoarding and expectations are now extreme.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Expect AI infrastructure names to trade sideways with choppy earnings reactions; a slowdown, not a bubble bursting, with hyperscalers more at risk than picks-and-shovels names.
insights.quote: I've been talking about an AI midcycle slowdown ... a lot of these semiconductor names ... we're probably going to have equal news of disappointment with equal news of surprises

themes.theme: Demand exponential and monetization mechanics for AI

insights.claim: AI demand is going exponential while supply is not responding: at Sylvia, queries per user per week jumped from six to fifteen simply as models got smarter, not from UX changes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Per-user engagement rising with model capability means the cost/capacity problem worsens over time, not improves; demand intensity is a leading indicator.
insights.quote: It used to be that people ask six questions per week per user. ... It's now 15.

insights.claim: AI labs will monetize via airline-style tiering, Uber-style surge pricing, ads served during query wait times, and steering casual users to cheaper or open-source models to free capacity for high-margin agentic and inference customers.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Expect price discrimination and ad-supported tiers as the next phase of AI business models; high-value verticals (e.g. pharma) get prioritized capacity.
insights.quote: You'll have surge pricing like, "Hey, just like you do with Uber."

insights.claim: Nvidia's open-source Nemotron model ranks surprisingly high and could run locally on laptops and phones, validating that many tasks don't need frontier models.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: On-device open models are a real off-ramp from frontier-model spend, reinforcing commoditization and edge-compute themes.

insights.claim: Coding capability is the leading path to AGI, and with Blackwell and Vera Rubin coming online it will accelerate; Dario Amodei's latest essay marks a 'critical point.'
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Watch coding benchmarks as the key AGI proxy; further acceleration supports both compute demand and Anthropic's competitive position.

insights.claim: Visser bets Anthropic wins the frontier-model battle over OpenAI because Anthropic chose enterprise and coding (the path to AGI) while OpenAI chose the harder consumer/Apple-style path.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Strategic positioning, not just model quality, frames Anthropic as the long-term winner; relevant for valuing both companies' pending offerings.
insights.quote: OpenAI has an uphill battle because they made a bad decision. They tried to choose the consumer Apple side as opposed to the enterprise side.

insights.claim: Visser predicts Sam Altman will not be running OpenAI within a year, citing both a likely loss of appetite for the role and possible pressure to step down.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A leadership-change call that, if right, would be a major OpenAI overhang ahead of an IPO; a concrete event to watch.
insights.quote: I would be shocked if with within a year if he's still running OpenAI.

insights.claim: Anthropic is winning the talent war (e.g. Andrej Karpathy joining), reinforcing its lead in coding and the race to AGI.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Talent concentration is a leading indicator of model leadership; supports the Anthropic-over-OpenAI thesis.

insights.claim: Jeff Bezos's new company Prometheus raised ~$12B at a ~$40B valuation to use AI plus hardware to collapse manufacturing timelines (e.g. work that takes 10,000 people 10 years done by 100 people in a year).
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Top-tier capital is flowing to the physical/manufacturing-AI bottleneck; a signal of where the next AI build-out value accrues.

insights.claim: The physical hardware and manufacturing side is now the bottleneck, and bottlenecks mean inflation, margin and demand, which is why MIT grads and Silicon Valley capital are pivoting toward physical AI.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Invest where the bottleneck (and thus pricing power) is: physical/hardware AI rather than commoditizing software layers.
insights.quote: the bottlenecks are real and he's trying to get a company that is to help with the bottlenecks regardless of where it is.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly will stop disclosing phase-one pharma data because AI lets competitors catch up quickly, signaling AI is compressing R&D timelines and changing competitive disclosure across industries.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: AI-driven catch-up risk is reshaping pharma IP strategy; a non-obvious read-through on how AI alters competitive moats beyond tech.

themes.theme: Crypto, blockchain authenticity and AI slop

insights.claim: As AI generates billions of deepfakes and synthetic content, blockchain becomes critical infrastructure for proving authenticity and provenance of real-world items and experiences.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames blockchain/NFTs as an authenticity layer in an AI-saturated world, a demand driver independent of the speculative art narrative.
insights.quote: in a world where you don't know what's real on anything, I fundamentally believe the blockchain and crypto is critical to all this stuff.

insights.claim: Visser frames crypto utility as a three-legged stool of stablecoins, tokenization and NFTs, with experiential/community value (e.g. event provenance) as an under-appreciated NFT use case.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A structured way to evaluate crypto network effects beyond price; tokenized provenance and on-chain authenticity are the bullish vectors he watches.
insights.quote: stable coins, tokenization, and NFTTS as the three stools of the utilization and the network effects of crypto.

insights.claim: Physical commemorative items rely on scarcity that can be instantly counterfeited, whereas blockchain provides verifiable proof of attendance/ownership; demonstrated by game-four Knicks commemorative tickets selling for $300-450 on eBay within a day.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Real-world demand for verifiable scarcity supports the on-chain provenance thesis; physical collectibles are vulnerable to fakes that blockchain solves.
insights.quote: if these things are selling on eBay for any amount of money, there'll be a flood of fakes in 2 seconds made

insights.claim: Bitcoin is being ignored because it trades well below its 200-day moving average, illustrating that investors chase 52-week-high narratives rather than buy weakness.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Contrarian framing: under-owned assets below trend (Bitcoin, silver) are where edge sits while the crowd chases momentum names.
insights.quote: Nobody wants to buy Bitcoin right now because it's way below the 200 day moving average.

insights.claim: Autonomous looped software building is here: a salesperson can have Claude transcribe a customer call and build the requested features in real time, demoing a working product before the call ends.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Real-time build-during-sales-call is a step-change in software-creation productivity; a concrete AI use case reshaping sales and dev economics.
insights.quote: By the end of the call, I was able to show a fully working product with the exact workflow they mentioned 15 minutes earlier.

insights.claim: Career advice in the AI era is that academic silos are over; the winning strategy is to become an AI-native worker who uses AI daily across functions rather than staying within one major or job.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Human-capital thesis: AI-native generalists outcompete narrow specialists, a lens for evaluating both careers and the firms that hire them.
insights.quote: There are no more silos, guys. Like the silos are over.
